### Organic & Biomolecular Chemistry

## Recent developments in the synthesis and applications of chiral ferrocene ligands and organocatalysts in asymmetric catalysis

### Abstract

With the ever-present need for novel asymmetric methodologies in organic synthesis to allow chemists to access enantiopure compounds, so too is there a need for new chiral ligands and organocatalysts to affect these transformations. Due to the unique properties of ferrocene and its derivatives, ferrocenyl compounds are unsurprisingly considered privileged structures in asymmetric catalysis. The versatility of the ferrocene moiety gives rise to a vast range of diverse ligand and catalyst motifs which are successful in broad ranges of mechanistically distinct reactions. This review details recent advances in the application of ferrocenyl mono-, bi- and tridentate-ligands and ferrocene-derived organocatalysts in asymmetric catalysis. Given the necessity for the development of new approaches to expand the scope of ferrocenyl ligands and catalysts, this review also illustrates the development of elegant and novel methodologies for the synthesis of ferrocenyl compounds.

--- SOURCE 3: Recent Advances in Catalytic Asymmetric Synthesis ---
URL: https://chiralpedia.com/blog/recent-advances-in-catalytic-asymmetric-synthesis/

SUMMARY:
By facilitating the formation of chiral molecules with high enantioselectivity, catalytic asymmetric synthesis has revolutionized the production of pharmaceuticals, agrochemicals, and fine chemicals. This blog explores recent advances in catalytic asymmetric synthesis, highlighting innovations in chiral catalysts, notable reactions, and their industrial applications. Asymmetric organocatalysis, which involves using small chiral organic molecules as catalysts for stereoselective reactions, has become a crucial pillar in asymmetric catalysis, alongside enzymes and metal-based catalysts. The field of catalytic asymmetric synthesis has seen remarkable advancements in recent years, driven by innovations in chiral catalyst design and a deeper understanding of reaction mechanisms. These advances have expanded the scope of asymmetric synthesis, enabling the efficient production of chiral compounds for various industrial applications.

Recent Advances in Catalytic Asymmetric Synthesis
=================================================

[2 Comments](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/recent-advances-in-catalytic-asymmetric-synthesis/#comments) / [Asymmetric Synthesis](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/category/asymmetric-synthesis/), [Chiral synthesis](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/category/chiral-synthesis/), [Enantioselective synthesis](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/category/enantioselective-synthesis/) / By [Valliappan Kannappan](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/author/chiralusrblg/ "View all posts by Valliappan Kannappan") / [#asymmetric_synthesis](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/tag/asymmetric_synthesis/), [#chirality](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/tag/chirality/), [#chiralpedia](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/tag/chiralpedia/)

**Introduction**

[Catalysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Catalysis) is a cornerstone of modern chemistry, playing a pivotal role in [asymmetric synthesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enantioselective_synthesis). By facilitating the formation of [chiral](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chirality) molecules with high enantioselectivity, catalytic asymmetric synthesis has revolutionized the production of [pharmaceuticals](https://www.britannica.com/technology/pharmaceutical), [agrochemicals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Agrochemical), and fine chemicals. The development of efficient [chiral catalysts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enantioselective_synthesis) has enabled chemists to produce [enantiomerically pure compounds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enantiopure_drug) more reliably and sustainably. This blog explores recent advances in catalytic asymmetric synthesis, highlighting innovations in chiral catalysts, notable reactions, and their industrial applications.

**Asymmetric/****Chiral Catalysts**

[Asymmetric catalysts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enantioselective_synthesis), also known as chiral catalysts, are substances that can selectively promote chemical reactions in a way that produces a single enantiomer of a chiral molecule.

Chiral catalysis is presently the most efficient approach for achieving chiral synthesis. The search for new, effective chiral catalysts and ligands for enantiopure molecules is a continuous effort in the pharmaceutical, flavors and fragrances, and agrochemical industries. Approximately 85% of newly introduced pharmaceuticals exhibit chirality. This requirement has resulted in advancements in the field of asymmetric synthesis of chiral ligands and metal complexes.

Representative asymmetric catalytic systems include: Enzymes (Biocatalysts), Metal complexes, Small organic molecules, which are collectively referred to as three types of pillar-type asymmetric catalysts.

![Image 2](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Three-PIllars.png)

Source: [http://www.beilstein-institut.de/Bozen2008/Proceedings/List/List.pdf](http://www.beilstein-institut.de/Bozen2008/Proceedings/List/List.pdf)[https://www.researchgate.net/publication/238658364](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/238658364)

The three pillars of asymmetric catalysis: Biocatalyis, Metal Catalysis and Organocatalysis

Biocatalysts

[Enzymes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme)are nature’s chiral catalysts, and their application in synthetic chemistry has grown significantly. Advances in protein engineering and directed evolution have enabled the creation of enzymes with enhanced activity and enantioselectivity for non-natural substrates. These [biocatalysts](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enzyme) offer high specificity and can operate under environmentally benign conditions, making them attractive for industrial applications.

Although enzymatic methods have been used in fermentation for thousands of years, their molecular-level significance was first recognized in the early 1800s when scientists began studying yeasts for their fermentation capabilities. Initially, it was thought that the entire microorganism acted as the catalyst. However, the discovery of the first enzyme mixture “diastase” revolutionized the field by showing that specific parts of the organism were responsible for the reactions. This breakthrough ignited interest in enzymology, leading to key developments such as the lock and key model, cell-free fermentation, the understanding that enzymes are proteins, and the discovery of DNA structure. These milestones have laid the foundation for modern biocatalysis. The image below provides an overview.

![Image 3](https://chiralpedia.com/blog/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/BIOCATAYSIS-OVERVIEW.png)

Source: : Image adopted from ACS Cent. Sci. 2021, 7, 1105−1116 

Biocatalysis has transcended early applications in fermentation to have an expanding footprint as an essential tool in the production of high value compounds. 

_**A**. Early Application: Early uses of enzyme-mediated transformations, such as fermentation, chiral resolutions, and functional group interconversions.

**B**. Increased Enzyme Accessibility: Recent advances in genome sequencing, gene synthesis, and bioinformatics increase the accessibility of obtaining enzymes. 

**C**. Current Biocatalysis: Select strategies in modern biocatalysis include cascades, chemoenzymatic synthesis, and enzyme evolution.

**D**. Future of Biocatalysis: Application of computational methods for Biocatalysis._

[Biocatalysis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biocatalysis) is widely acknowledged as a well-established technique for asymmetric synthesis, owing to the remarkable selectivity (enantio-, chemo-, and regio-) that enzymes frequently exhibit in various chemical reactions. The field has undergone significant advancement with the integration of emerging Molecular Biology techniques, which are utilized for the production and design of enzymes, along with the incorporation of process-development concepts (such as transitioning from batch to continuous reactors) and medium engineering strategies (such as non-aqueous media and immobilization). Biocatalysis can meet the requirements of contemporary organic synthesis, which necessitates a combination of high selectivity and efficiency, as well as sustainability and cost-effectiveness. Consequently, there has been a growing implementation of various industrial processes, which include a wide range of examples utilizing free enzymes, whole cells, systems biocatalysis, and multi-step processes.

![Image 4](https://pub.mdpi-res.com/catalysts/catalysts-09-00802/article_deploy/html/images/catalysts-09-00802-ag-550.jpg?1572350509)

Image Credit: _Catalysts_**2019**,_9_(10), 802;[https://doi.org/10.3390/catal9100802](https://doi.org/10.3390/catal9100802)

Below is a depiction of a patent survey conducted between 2014 and 2019, which provides more detailed information about the anticipated advancements in biocatalysis for asymmetric synthesis. The data accurately depict the contemporary trends in the field, sourced from academic and industrial groups, and encompassing various enzymes and chemical processes. Surprisingly, granted patents can offer additional evidence of the essential requirements of novelty, innovation, and resolution of industrial problems, which are necessary for intellectual property (IP) rights..

![Image 5](https://www.mdpi.com/catalysts/catalysts-09-00802/article_deploy/html/images/catalysts-09-00802-g001-550.jpg)

Source: _Catalysts_**2019**,_9_(10), 802;[https://doi.org/10.3390/catal9100802](https://doi.org/10.3390/catal9100802)

Patents granted in 2014–2019

Around 200 patents related to biocatalysis have been successfully granted within the last five years

Enzymes have been utilized for a diverse range of chemical processes for many years. Enzymes possess significant potency due to their ability to combine the benefits of a directing group that controls selectivity and a catalyst into a single reagent. Moreover, they can be employed alongside other enzymes in a one-pot reaction. In the last two decades, the integration of synthetic and enzymatic systems has facilitated numerous total synthesis projects. Moreover, enzymes are increasingly being employed as a standard practice in certain process chemistry teams within the industry. Previously, only a limited number of enzymes, such as lipases or ketoreductases (KREDs), were accessible for use in chemical synthesis. Nevertheless, the expansion of potential enzyme sources for applications in process chemistry has quickened, leading to a wide range of enzymes now accessible to researchers. The possibility of ‘bio-retrosynthesis’ was demonstrated in 2014 with the development of a complete enzymatic synthesis of the nucleoside didanosine. Retrosynthesis is a concept that involves breaking bonds in a target molecule to create simpler precursors using synthetic transformations. In the case of bio-retrosynthesis, an artificial [enzyme cascade](https://www.nature.com/articles/s43586-021-00044-z#Glos1) is designed as a synthetic biochemical pathway. This cascade utilizes enzymes as catalysts to achieve the necessary chemical reactions, providing a potential pathway to produce the desired target molecule.

Biocatalysis is facilitated by several factors, such as the design of the reaction, the selection and optimization of the biocatalyst, and the development of the bioprocess. It has been employed in the production of a diverse array of chemicals and pharmaceuticals, including the ones depicted in the Figure below.

![Image 6](https://media.springernature.com/full/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs43586-021-00044-z/MediaObjects/43586_2021_44_Fig1_HTML.png?as=webp)

Source: Nat Rev Methods Primers, 2021, [https://doi.org/10.1038/s43586-021-00044-z](https://doi.org/10.1038/s43586-021-00044-z)

Examples of different products synthesized using biocatalysis

_For instance, nitrile hydratases are employed on a large scale, producing acrylamide in quantities of thousands of tons. Additionally, enzymes have been incorporated into detergents for over three decades. In recent times, there has been a growing prevalence in the utilization of proteins as catalysts in the chemical synthesis of intricate compounds, including pharmaceuticals. The synthesis of the HIV inhibitor islatravir, driven entirely by biocatalysts, showcases the effectiveness of integrating contemporary methods in designing novel enzyme cascades. These methods include repurposing established biosynthetic pathways, screening saturation mutagenesis libraries to identify enzyme variants, and employing directed evolution techniques to enhance enzyme stability and turnover._

Transition Metal Catalysts

[Transition metal](https://simple.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transition_metal)complexes are among the most versatile and widely used chiral catalysts in asymmetric synthesis. These complexes often feature a central metal atom coordinated to chiral ligands that create an asymmetric environment around the active site. This setup facilitates enantioselective reactions. Recent advances include the development of new chiral ligands that enhance the selectivity and efficiency of metal-catalyzed processes.

**Knowles’s catalytic asymmetric hydrogenation**

Similar to how the left hand is a reflection of the right hand, numerous molecules exist in two forms that have the same chemical composition but are mirror images of each other. The two forms can exhibit distinct characteristics and have contrasting impacts on the body. For instance, a particular variant of the limonene molecule emits an aroma reminiscent of oranges, while its reflection possesses a lemony scent with hints of turpentine.

An additional instance is the medication [L-dopa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/L-DOPA). One variant, referred to as L, mitigates symptoms associated with [Parkinson’s disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease), such as tremors and rigidity. However, it is also available in an alternative form known as D-dopa, which possesses toxic properties. In the 1960s, the conventional procedure for producing L-dopa resulted in an equal quantity of both forms. The process of separating them after their creation proved to be both time-consuming and costly.

[Dr. Knowles](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/knowles/facts/) devised a method to optimize the manufacturing process in order to increase the production of the most sought-after variant of specific molecules, such as L-dopa. The tool he used was a catalyst, which is a substance frequently employed to accelerate a chemical reaction. He pioneered the technique of asymmetric hydrogenation, employing a catalyst to not only accelerate the reaction but also bias it towards the production of 97.5 percent L-dopa and only 2.5 percent of the undesired (D) form. Subsequently, Monsanto initiated extensive manufacturing of the medication, which continues to be a fundamental component in the treatment of [Parkinson’s disease](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parkinson%27s_disease), particularly during its initial phases.

**Knowles’ Pioneering Work** and its Industrial application

![Image 7](https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/chempubeng4high.jpg)

Source: [https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/popular-information/](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/popular-information/)

The first industrial catalytic asymmetric synthesis

_Knowles and his colleagues utilized compound C as the initial substance in their industrial production of L-DOPA through synthesis. DiPAMP, one of the enantiomers, was utilized in the chiral hydrogenation process. Out of the product, the enantiomer D accounted for 97.5%. Subsequently, L-DOPA was obtained through the acid hydrolysis of D._ _Knowles’ objective was to create a method for producing the amino acid L-DOPA on an industrial scale, as it had demonstrated its effectiveness in treating Parkinson’s disease. Through experimentation with different structural variations of phosphines, Knowles and his colleagues efficiently generated effective catalysts that exhibited a high level of enantiomeric excess, specifically favoring L-DOPA. The ligand subsequently employed in Monsanto’s industrial synthesis of L-DOPA was the diphosphine ligand DiPAMP. A rhodium complex, when combined with the ligand shown in the image above, produced both enantiomers of DOPA in a 100% yield. The product consisted of 97.5% L-DOPA. Knowles quickly and successfully utilized his own fundamental research and the research of others to develop an industrial process for synthesizing a drug._

**Noyori’s general hydrogenation catalysts**

[Ryoji Noyori](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/noyori/facts/), a Japanese scientist, has conducted extensive and intensive research and developed better and more general catalysts for hydrogenation. The implications of his research are highly significant.

In 1980, Noyori and his colleagues published an article illustrating the synthesis of both enantiomers of the diphosphine ligand BINAP, as shown in the Figure below. These catalysts, when combined with rhodium complexes, facilitate the production of specific amino acids with a chiral excess of up to 100%. Takasago International industry has been utilizing BINAP in the industrial synthesis of the chiral aroma compound menthol since the early 1980s.

_Noyori saw the need for more general catalysts with broader applications. Exchanging rhodium, Rh(I), for another transition metal, ruthenium, Ru(II), proved, for example, to be successful. The ruthenium(II)-BINAP complex hydrogenates many types of molecules with other functional groups. These reactions give a high enantiomeric excess and high yields and can be scaled up for industrial use._

Noyori’s catalysts have been extensively utilized in the synthesis of fine chemicals, pharmaceutical products, and novel advanced materials. Noyori recognized the necessity for more versatile catalysts that could be applied to a wider range of situations. The substitution of rhodium, Rh(I), with ruthenium, Ru(II), was found to be successful. The ruthenium(II)-BINAP complex is capable of catalyzing the hydrogenation of various molecules that contain different functional groups. These reactions exhibit a significant enantiomeric excess and high yields, making them suitable for industrial-scale production.

**Sharpless’ chirally catalyzed oxidations**

In addition to the progress made in chirally catalyzed hydrogenation reactions, [Barry Sharpless](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/sharpless/facts/) has also developed chiral catalysts specifically designed for other significant reactions, such as oxidations. Hydrogenation eliminates a functional group by saturating the double bond, whereas oxidation results in an increase in functionality. This opens up new opportunities for constructing intricate molecules.

Sharpless recognized a significant demand for catalysts to facilitate asymmetric oxidations. His [chiral epoxidation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sharpless_epoxidation) serves as an example of the several significant discoveries he has made. In 1980, he conducted successful experiments that resulted in the development of a practical technique for the catalytic asymmetric oxidation of allylic alcohols, leading to the production of chiral epoxides. This reaction employed the transition metal titanium (Ti) and chiral ligands, resulting in a high enantiomeric excess. Epoxides serve as valuable intermediate compounds in a wide range of synthesis processes. This approach has facilitated significant variations in structure and has been extensively utilized in both academic and industrial research. The chemical reaction for the production of the epoxide (R)-glycidol is illustrated below.

**Sharpless’s Work** and its Industrial Application

The emergence of the powerful Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation in the 1980s has stimulated major advances in both academic and industrial organic synthesis. Through the action of an enantiomerically pure titanium-tartrate complex, a myriad of achiral and

chiral allylic alcohols can be epoxidized with exceptional stereoselectivity. Interest in the Sharpless epoxidation as a tool for industrial organic synthesis increased substantially after Sharpless et al. had discovered that the asymmetric epoxidation process can be

conducted with catalytic amounts of the enantiomerically pure titanium-tartrate complex simply by adding molecular sieves to the epoxidation reaction mixture. Using this practical and reproducible catalytic variant, an industrial process for ton-scale productions of (S)- and (R)-glycidol and (S)- and (R)-methylglycidol has been developed. Many scientists have identified Sharpless’ epoxidation as the most important discovery in the field of synthesis

![Image 10](https://www.nobelprize.org/uploads/2018/06/chempubeng7high.jpg)

Source: [https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/popular-information/](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2001/popular-information/)

Glycidol is employed in the pharmaceutical sector for the synthesis of beta-blockers, 

used as cardiovascular medications. 

_Sharpless’ epoxidation has been widely recognized by scientists as a pivotal breakthrough in the synthesis field. The allylic alcohol undergoes oxidation to form the epoxide (R)-glycidol, utilizing the oxidizing agent tertiary butylhydroperoxide in the presence of a catalyst. This catalyst is synthesized by combining titanium tetraisopropoxide with the diethyl ester of naturally occurring D-tartaric acid in the reaction mixture. The metal binds the chiral ligand, hyperperoxide, and substrate simultaneously, leading to the chiral epoxidation reaction._

Organocatalysts

Asymmetric [organocatalysis](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2021/popular-information/), which involves using small chiral organic molecules as catalysts for stereoselective reactions, has become a crucial pillar in asymmetric catalysis, alongside enzymes and metal-based catalysts. This approach has found its way into the toolkits of scientists working on both academic and industrial projects. Over the past two decades, the field has introduced unique concepts and methods, earning recognition through prestigious awards, including the 2021 Nobel Prize in Chemistry awarded to [Benjamin List](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2021/list/facts/) and [David MacMillan](https://www.nobelprize.org/prizes/chemistry/2021/macmillan/facts/) for their pioneering work in this area – “_the development of asymmetric organocatalysis_”. While the use of organic compounds as catalysts has been known for over a century, it was the groundbreaking publications by List, Barbas III, Lerner, and MacMillan in 2000 that sparked a new trend in organic synthesis. Thanks to MacMillan, the term “organocatalysis” has become widely accepted, leading to a significant increase in research groups focusing on this field.

It’s important to note that although the 2000 publications marked the beginning of modern organocatalysis and generated significant interest and competition in organic synthesis, there were several important contributions made earlier. These earlier works did not receive widespread attention at the time and were seen as isolated catalytic methods rather than foundational concepts for a broader field. Additionally, these earlier approaches were not classified as organocatalysis because the term did not exist then.

The story of asymmetric organocatalysis is truly amazing. The milestones in the historical developments are presented in the scheme below.

Selected milestones in the historical development of asymmetric organocatalysis.

_The journey of asymmetric organocatalysis has been nothing short of remarkable. It all began over a century ago with the pioneering work of scientists like von Liebig, Knoevenagel, and Bredig, who demonstrated that small chiral organic molecules could catalyze asymmetric reactions. Fast forward to the latter half of the 20th century, and we saw the first highly enantioselective reactions being reported, showcasing the potential of this field. The real excitement, however, kicked off in 2000 with groundbreaking publications by MacMillan and List. Their work sparked a wave of interest and innovation in asymmetric organocatalysis, leading to rapid advancements and widespread recognition. The precise construction of chiral molecules is a core research area in chemical science and technology. Among the plethora of reported synthetic methods, asymmetric catalysis has emerged as one of the most efficient and direct ways to obtain optically pure chiral compounds. This article mainly reviews the recent progress of asymmetric catalysis made by Chinese chemists from the following aspects: the development of new chiral ligands, transition metal-catalyzed asymmetric reactions, asymmetric organocatalytic reactions, and new strategies for asymmetric catalysis. This review does not intend to be comprehensive; only representative examples are selected to highlight the important progress made in this field. Finally, a brief outlook is given on the development of novel chiral ligands/catalysts, new enantioselective reactions, and their applications in chiral drugs and agricultural chemicals.

Introduction
------------

The precise construction of multilevel chiral substances is a core research area in chiral science that provides a crucial technology in the fields of medicine, pesticides, informatics, and materials. The development of new methods that allow for the access of structurally diverse chiral compounds in a precise and practical manner lays the foundation for advanced innovation in this field. Among the plethora of reported synthetic methods, asymmetric catalytic reactions employing chiral catalysts are the most efficient ways to obtain optically pure chiral compounds. Over the past several decades, significant progress has been achieved in asymmetric catalysis. Thousands of chiral ligands, chiral metal catalysts, and small-molecule organocatalysts have been meticulously designed and synthesized. At the same time, a myriad of asymmetric catalytic reactions, as well as new catalytic methods and strategies, have been devised to enable the efficient construction of C–H, C–C, and C–X bonds in a highly enantioselective manner. These advancements have found successful applications in the asymmetric synthesis of natural products and functional materials, as well as the industrial preparation of chiral drugs and pesticides. Knowles, Noyori, and Sharpless were awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2001 for their outstanding contributions to transition metal-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation and oxidation reactions. In addition, List and MacMillan were honored with the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 2021 for their exceptional contributions to the development of asymmetric organocatalysis.

Research on chiral science and technology in China commenced in the 1980s and began to garner significant attention in the 1990s. In 1995, Dai et al.[1](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r1) published a review article, introducing the latest progress of chiral science and technology in the world at that time. Recognizing the significance of chiral science and technology, the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC) launched major research projects: “Chemical and Biological Research on Chiral Drugs” and “Study on Scientific Issues in Chirality and Chiral Drug Research” during the Ninth and Tenth Five-Year Plans.[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r2),[3](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r3) Furthermore, in 2018, the NSFC initiated a major research plan titled “Precise Construction of Multilevel Chiral Substances.” Concurrently, the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Ministry of Education have also deployed a number of key and major research projects in the field of asymmetric catalysis. Over the past two decades, great progress has been made in the field of chiral science and technology in China.[4](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r4) This review mainly summarizes the research advancements in asymmetric catalysis made by Chinese chemists in recent years, encompassing the development of chiral ligands, transition metal-catalyzed enantioselective reactions, organocatalytic enantioselective reactions, and new strategies for asymmetric catalysis.

Chiral Ligands
--------------

Chiral ligands and catalysts are the central focus in the research area of asymmetric catalytic synthesis.[5](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r5) In fact, every breakthrough made in this field is mainly related to the appearance of a new chiral ligand and/or catalyst. Studies on the development of new chiral ligands and catalysts in China began at the end of last century. Several remarkable chiral ligands were reported around the year 2000. For example, Dai et al.[6](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r6) developed chiral oxazoline-type ligands for the first time and studied their chiral induction ability in asymmetric dihydroxylation of alkenes (Scheme[1](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf1)). Chan et al.[7](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r7) disclosed a chiral diphosphinite ligand (SpirOP) based on a rigid spiro skeleton and its successful application in the rhodium-catalyzed homogeneous asymmetric hydrogenation of α-dehydroamino acid derivatives in 1997. Thereafter, Wu and Chan[8](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r8) developed another chiral bisphosphine (P-Phos) ligand featuring a 3,3′-bipyridine framework. In addition to its good chemical stability in air, this ligand exhibited comparable catalytic properties to that of 2,2’-bis(diphenylphosphino)-1,1’-binaphthyl (BINAP) in asymmetric hydrogenation reactions.[8](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r8) Dai and coworkers[9](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r9) developed a series of novel ferrocene ligands (SIOCPhox). Excellent regio-, diastereo-, and enantioselectivities were achieved in the asymmetric allylic substitution and Heck reactions.[9](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r9) Tang and coworkers[10](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r10) developed a side arm strategy for the design of chiral ligands/catalysts. Some tunable pseudo-_C_ 3-symmetric chiral tri(oxazoline) and side armed bis(oxazoline) ligands were thus prepared, which showed superior catalytic properties in various catalytic reactions in comparison to their analogous bis(oxazoline) ligands.[10](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r10) Following these inspiring achievements, more and more fantastic chiral ligands have been designed and synthesized. Some representative examples will be described in the following section.

### Chiral spiro ligands

Spirocyclic structures are commonly found in nature. The inherent molecular rigidity and the restricted orientation of the quaternary bridging carbon atom make them ideal backbones for chiral ligand design. Since the 1990s, chiral spiro skeletons have attracted increasing attention and gradually developed into a unique and privileged ligand structure, from spiro[4.4]nonane with three chiral centers to spirobiindane and spiro[4.4]nonadiene with only one axial chirality (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)). To date, a large variety of chiral spiro ligands with different skeletons have been developed and extensively applied in various transition metal-catalyzed asymmetric reactions, affording high catalytic activity and excellent enantioselectivity.

![Image 5: Scheme 33](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/specs/ux3/releasedAssets/images/loader-7e60691fbe777356dc81ff6d223a82a6.gif)

Scheme 2 | Representative chiral spiro ligands.

*   [Download figure](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/cms/asset/83083e78-6b56-44ea-9bc5-dd38fa1e0b68/sf2.gif)
*   [Download PowerPoint](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/action/downloadFigures?id=sf2&doi=10.31635%2Fccschem.023.202303347)

Zhou and coworkers[11](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r11) designed and synthesized the first spirobiindane-based chiral spiro ligand, SIPHOS, in 2002 by incorporating benzo rings into the spiro[4.4]nonane structure, thus reducing the number of chiral centers and increasing the rigidity and tunability of the ligand. Encouraged by the success of SIPHOS in a series of Rh-, Ir-, and Ni-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation and coupling reactions, a family of spirobiindane-based chiral ligands have been constructed, including various mono-, bi-, and tridentate phosphorus-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-containing ligands (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)). All of these ligands have been intensively investigated in asymmetric hydrogenation, carbon–carbon bond forming reactions, and carbon–heteroatom bond forming reactions for the preparation of different types of chiral compounds including chiral natural products and chiral drugs.[12](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r12)[](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r13)[](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r14)–[15](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r15)

In 2009, Ding and coworkers[16](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r16) reported spiro[4.4]-1,6-nonadiene-based chiral phosphine-oxazoline ligands (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)) by introducing C=C double bonds into the cyclic framework of spiro[4.4]nonane for the first time. These rigid chiral spiro ligands were successfully utilized in the Ir-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of some challenging substrates containing C=N and C=C double bonds.[16](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r16),[17](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r17)

In addition to the chiral spiro ligands with an all-carbon spirocyclic backbone described above, heteroatoms (_O_ and _N_) have been introduced into the spiro cyclic backbone to generate new types of chiral ligands bearing a hetero-spiro framework. Inspired by the pioneering work of an aromatic spiroketal-based diphosphine (SPANphos) reported by van Leeuwen and coworkers,[18](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r18) Ding and coworkers[19](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r19) realized the practical synthesis of structurally adaptable spiroketal-based chiral diphosphine (SKP) ligands in the year of 2012 (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)), featuring an extraordinarily long P···P distance, a flexible backbone, and unusually large bite angles in their transition metal complexes. These SKP ligands have found diverse utility in several Cu-, Au-, Pd-, and Rh-catalyzed asymmetric reactions and provided outstanding performance in some challenging asymmetric transformations.[20](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r20)

By introducing oxygen atoms into the spirobiindane skeleton, Zhang and coworkers[21](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r21) synthesized several chiral oxa-spirocyclic ligands (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)), such as tridentate _O_-SpiroPAP and diphosphine ligand _O_-SDP, showing high levels of activity and enantioselectivity in the Ru-catalyzed asymmetric hydrogenation of α,β-unsaturated carboxylic acids.

Most recently, a novel kind of chiral aza-spiro ligand based on a spirocyclic pyrrolidine oxazoline backbone (SPDO ligands) was designed and synthesized by Tu and coworkers[22](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r22),[23](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r23) (Scheme[2](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf2)). These ligands have shown high efficiency and excellent enantioselectivity in challenging Cu-catalyzed direct asymmetric aerobic oxidative homo-coupling of 2-naphthols and cross-coupling of 2-naphthylamines with 2-naphthols to deliver a wide scope of _C_ 1-symmetric chiral binaphthol (BINOL) and 3,3′-disubstituted 2-amino-2’-hydroxy-1,1’-binaphthyl (NOBIN) derivatives.[22](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r22),[23](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r23)

To sum up, all of this remarkable progress demonstrates that the chiral spiro skeleton is a privileged structural motif and will continue to flourish in future studies of asymmetric catalysis.

### Chiral _N_,_N_′-dioxide-based ligands

Chiral amine _N_-oxides feature an electron-donating oxygen atom in the N+-O− group and have proved to be effective ligands and organocatalysts in asymmetric catalysis since 1993.[24](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r24) On the basis of their initial work by using chiral _N_-oxides in asymmetric silylcyanation and Strecker reactions and inspired by the concept of bifunctional catalysis,[25](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r25) Feng and coworkers invented a new family of _C_ 2-symmetric _N_,_N_′-dioxide amides from readily available chiral amino acids (Scheme[3](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#sf3)).[26](https://www.chinesechemsoc.org/doi/10.31635/ccschem.023.202303347#r26) These chiral tertiary amines, _N_,_N_′-dioxides, are operationally simple and relatively insensitive to moisture

enriched_points.expanded_content: Recent innovations in chiral ligands and organocatalysts, particularly ferrocenyl derivatives, have enhanced asymmetric synthesis, enabling the efficient production of enantiomerically pure compounds.
The 2001 Nobel Prize acknowledged Knowles, Noyori, and Sharpless for their pivotal work in asymmetric synthesis, revolutionizing pharmaceutical development and industrial applications.
Biocatalysis leverages enzymes for asymmetric synthesis, offering high specificity and efficiency, essential for producing complex pharmaceuticals sustainably and cost-effectively.
